title: What are method references in Java 8, and how do they differ from lambda expressions?

answers.junior: Method references in Java 8 are syntactic sugar that provide a compact way to invoke existing methods by referencing them directly, rather than wrapping them in lambda expressions. They are defined using the double colon operator (::) and can reference static methods, instance methods, constructors, or methods from a particular object.
answers.middle: While both method references and lambda expressions are functional interfaces and can be used interchangeably in many contexts, method references offer more readability and clarity when you're simply delegating to an existing method, whereas lambda expressions are better suited for implementing new logic inline. There are four types of method references: static method references like Integer::parseInt or Math::max, instance method references from a specific object like "str"::length, instance method references from any object of a class like String::length or String::compareTo, and constructor references like ArrayList::new. The key difference from lambda expressions is that method references don't create a new function body—they directly reference an already-defined method, making the code more readable and maintainable in scenarios like Collections.sort(list, String::compareTo) versus Collections.sort(list, (a, b) -> a.compareTo(b)).
answers.senior: Performance-wise, both are typically compiled to similar bytecode, though method references can sometimes allow more aggressive compiler and JVM optimizations since the compiler has explicit knowledge of the target method. Lambda expressions are more flexible for complex logic that can't be expressed as a simple method call, such as (x, y) -> x * 2 + y, while method references excel in common functional patterns like mapping, filtering, and sorting where you're delegating to an existing method. A practical example demonstrating the difference would be: list.stream().map(Integer::valueOf) is cleaner and more idiomatic than list.stream().map(s -> Integer.valueOf(s)), and list.stream().filter(s -> s.length() > 0) requires a lambda since there's no existing method that directly encodes this filtering logic.
answers.tricky: In production code, prefer method references when they improve readability and maintainability, but don't force them artificially—if a lambda expression is clearer or more self-documenting, use it instead.
